key_insight: By tuning chemical vapor transport growth conditions, the authors suppressed a prominent electrically active defect in CrSBr by up to an order of magnitude, with evidence pointing to a sulfur-related vacancy complex.
engineering_relevance: This provides a practical growth recipe for producing higher-quality CrSBr crystals with lower defect density, which is important for improving magneto-electrical device performance and reproducibility.
method: Correlated CVT growth conditions with defect densities measured by conductive atomic force microscopy (CAFM).
Varied precursor stoichiometry and growth temperature while keeping the temperature gradient fixed.
Used thermodynamic modeling and density functional theory to identify the likely atomic nature of the D* defect.
evidence_signal: CAFM observations showed the D* defect concentration dropped by up to 10× under sulfur- and bromine-rich, lower-temperature growth conditions.
watch_item: The defect assignment is model-based rather than directly atomically resolved, so the D* identification as a sulfur-related vacancy complex should be treated as a well-supported hypothesis pending further structural confirmation.
paper_title: Growth-controlled suppression of electrically active defects in CrSBr
